Natural: 
Awkward: 
Operator hesitation: 
Fixture realism:

inputObservationForm.learnerResponse: 공배수는 둘 다 담는 쪽이라 먼저 구조를 정하고 계산은 나중에 해야 해.

latestReplayResult.name: Active S5C transfer log with Korean text-only observation

messages.speaker: tutor
messages.text: 계산하기 전에, 이 문제가 겹치는 쪽인지 둘을 다 담는 쪽인지 먼저 말해 볼래?

messages.speaker: learner
messages.text: 이건 공배수라서 둘을 다 담는 쪽을 먼저 보고, 계산은 그다음에 해야 해.

turnResults.operatorNote: 튜터가 구조 구분 발화만 기록하고 체크박스는 비워 두었다.

nextRecommendedSession.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
watchFor: Learner keeps a composite factor like 6 in the final line.
Learner rewrites with only prime factors but drops one repeated factor.
nextRecommendedSession.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and rebuild the last line only with prime factors that still remain.

steps.title: Sort Common Divisor vs Common Multiple
steps.openingLine: Before calculating, let's decide what kind of structure this question is asking for.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: Are you looking for what overlaps or what covers both numbers?
steps.smallHint: Say the task type first: shared structure for common divisor, covering structure for common multiple.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner names the task type first and delays calculation until that structure is clear.

history.learnerResponse: 이건 공배수라서 둘을 다 담는 쪽을 먼저 보고, 계산은 그다음에 해야 해.
history.tutorNote: 튜터가 구조 구분 발화만 기록하고 체크박스는 비워 두었다.

observations: 이건 공배수라서 둘을 다 담는 쪽을 먼저 보고, 계산은 그다음에 해야 해.
튜터가 구조 구분 발화만 기록하고 체크박스는 비워 두었다.

latestRecommendations.targetSkillTitle: Use factor structure when comparing common divisors or common multiples
latestRecommendations.summary: Learner mixes common-divisor and common-multiple reasoning in transfer tasks.

recommendedActivities.title: Sort Common Divisor vs Common Multiple
recommendedActivities.goal: Help the learner name which structure to look for before calculating.
recommendedActivities.firstTutorQuestion: Are you looking for what overlaps or what covers both numbers?
recommendedActivities.operatorFocus: Get the learner to classify the structure before any arithmetic and keep them from computing too early.
watchFor: Learner starts calculating without deciding whether the task is GCD or LCM.
Learner uses the word 'common' but cannot distinguish shared from covering structure.
Learner classifies correctly once but then falls back into calculation-first behavior.
recommendedActivities.exampleTutorMove: Pause the learner at the first arithmetic move and ask them to restate whether this is an overlap task or a cover-both task.

steps.title: Rewrite the Final Factorization Cleanly
steps.openingLine: Your split-work is close; now let's turn it into one clean final factorization.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
steps.smallHint: Keep only prime factors in the last line, and make sure none were dropped.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner writes one complete prime factorization with no missing or composite factor.
watchFor: Learner keeps a composite factor like 6 in the final line.
Learner rewrites with only prime factors but drops one repeated factor.
steps.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and rebuild the last line only with prime factors that still remain.
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: The final line should keep 2, 2, and 3 because all of them are prime and none can be dropped.

steps.title: Sort Common Divisor vs Common Multiple
steps.openingLine: Before calculating, let's decide what kind of structure this question is asking for.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: Are you looking for what overlaps or what covers both numbers?
steps.smallHint: Say the task type first: shared structure for common divisor, covering structure for common multiple.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner names the task type first and delays calculation until that structure is clear.
watchFor: Learner starts calculation before deciding whether the task is about overlap or coverage.
Learner uses the same rule for both common divisor and common multiple.
Learner says 'common' but cannot explain whether they need shared factors or enough factors for both numbers.
steps.exampleTutorMove: Stop the arithmetic and ask, 'Before we compute, is this the overlap answer or the cover-both answer?'
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: A common divisor uses only the overlap, but a common multiple needs enough prime factors to cover both numbers.

blockedByPrerequisites.whyBlocked: Prerequisite skill U1-S3 does not yet have learner evidence, so progress on U1-S5C should stay conservative.

recommendedActivitySequence.title: Rewrite the Final Factorization Cleanly
recommendedActivitySequence.goal: Stabilize the move from split-work to a complete final prime factorization.
recommendedActivitySequence.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
recommendedActivitySequence.operatorFocus: Push for one clean final line, not more splitting work.
watchFor: Learner drops a repeated factor while rewriting.
Learner leaves a composite factor in the final line.
recommendedActivitySequence.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and ask the learner to rebuild only the final prime-factor line.

observationFormTemplate.learnerResponsePrompt: Write the learner's clean final prime-factor line.
observationFormTemplate.tutorNotePrompt: Note whether the learner kept all repeated factors and removed composite factors.

fields.label: Only prime factors
fields.prompt: Did the learner write the final line using only prime factors?
fields.trueMeans: The learner removed any composite factor from the final line.

fields.label: Keeps all factors
fields.prompt: Did the learner keep every needed factor, including repeats?
fields.trueMeans: The learner did not drop a repeated or needed factor.

observationFormTemplate.learnerResponsePrompt: Write how the learner described the difference between shared structure and covering structure.
observationFormTemplate.tutorNotePrompt: Note whether the learner named the task type before calculating.

fields.label: Names overlap vs cover
fields.prompt: Did the learner say whether the task asks for overlap or for covering both numbers?
fields.trueMeans: The learner classified the task before starting arithmetic.

fields.label: Delays calculation
fields.prompt: Did the learner hold off on arithmetic until the task type was named clearly?
fields.trueMeans: The learner kept calculation secondary until the structure was identified.

recommendedActivities.title: Rewrite the Final Factorization Cleanly
recommendedActivities.goal: Stabilize the move from split-work to a complete final prime factorization.
recommendedActivities.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
recommendedActivities.operatorFocus: Push for one clean final line, not more splitting work.
watchFor: Learner drops a repeated factor while rewriting.
Learner leaves a composite factor in the final line.
recommendedActivities.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and ask the learner to rebuild only the final prime-factor line.

steps.title: Entry Bridge With 24
steps.openingLine: Let's just find one easy way into the problem instead of solving everything at once.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: What is the easiest divisor you can test first?
steps.smallHint: If the number is even, checking 2 is a calm first step.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner starts with 2 for 24 and explains that 24 is even.
watchFor: Learner stalls because they think they need the whole tree immediately.
Learner notices 24 is even but still does not choose 2.
steps.exampleTutorMove: You do not need the whole answer yet. What does the word 'even' let you try first?
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: 24 is even, so trying 2 first is the easiest entry.

steps.title: Rewrite the Final Factorization Cleanly
steps.openingLine: Your split-work is close; now let's turn it into one clean final factorization.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
steps.smallHint: Keep only prime factors in the last line, and make sure none were dropped.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner writes one complete prime factorization with no missing or composite factor.
watchFor: Learner keeps a composite factor like 6 in the final line.
Learner rewrites with only prime factors but drops one repeated factor.
steps.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and rebuild the last line only with prime factors that still remain.
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: The final line should keep 2, 2, and 3 because all of them are prime and none can be dropped.

blockedByPrerequisites.whyBlocked: Prerequisite skill U1-S2 does not yet have learner evidence, so progress on U1-S3 should stay conservative.

recommendedActivitySequence.title: Entry Bridge With 24
recommendedActivitySequence.goal: Rebuild the habit of starting with an easy divisor pair instead of freezing.
recommendedActivitySequence.firstTutorQuestion: What is the easiest divisor you can test first?
recommendedActivitySequence.operatorFocus: Reward a calm starting move instead of speed.
watchFor: Learner freezes because they think they must finish the whole factorization at once.
Learner chooses a hard divisor first even after noticing the number is even.
recommendedActivitySequence.exampleTutorMove: If the learner hesitates, point back to the word 'even' and ask what divisor that suggests.

latestRecommendations.targetSkillTitle: Use factor structure when comparing common divisors or common multiples
latestRecommendations.summary: Learner mixes common-divisor and common-multiple reasoning in transfer tasks.

recommendedActivities.title: Sort Common Divisor vs Common Multiple
recommendedActivities.goal: Help the learner name which structure to look for before calculating.
recommendedActivities.firstTutorQuestion: Are you looking for what overlaps or what covers both numbers?
recommendedActivities.operatorFocus: Get the learner to classify the structure before any arithmetic and keep them from computing too early.
watchFor: Learner starts calculating without deciding whether the task is GCD or LCM.
Learner uses the word 'common' but cannot distinguish shared from covering structure.
Learner classifies correctly once but then falls back into calculation-first behavior.
recommendedActivities.exampleTutorMove: Pause the learner at the first arithmetic move and ask them to restate whether this is an overlap task or a cover-both task.

steps.title: Sort Common Divisor vs Common Multiple
steps.openingLine: Before calculating, let's decide what kind of structure this question is asking for.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: Are you looking for what overlaps or what covers both numbers?
steps.smallHint: Say the task type first: shared structure for common divisor, covering structure for common multiple.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner names the task type first and delays calculation until that structure is clear.
watchFor: Learner starts calculation before deciding whether the task is about overlap or coverage.
Learner uses the same rule for both common divisor and common multiple.
Learner says 'common' but cannot explain whether they need shared factors or enough factors for both numbers.
steps.exampleTutorMove: Stop the arithmetic and ask, 'Before we compute, is this the overlap answer or the cover-both answer?'
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: A common divisor uses only the overlap, but a common multiple needs enough prime factors to cover both numbers.

observationFormTemplate.learnerResponsePrompt: Write how the learner described the difference between shared structure and covering structure.
observationFormTemplate.tutorNotePrompt: Note whether the learner named the task type before calculating.

fields.label: Names overlap vs cover
fields.prompt: Did the learner say whether the task asks for overlap or for covering both numbers?
fields.trueMeans: The learner classified the task before starting arithmetic.

fields.label: Delays calculation
fields.prompt: Did the learner hold off on arithmetic until the task type was named clearly?
fields.trueMeans: The learner kept calculation secondary until the structure was identified.
